result:
# On the Strangeness of Doorways

I've been thinking about doorways. Not the metaphorical kind—the ones writers love, the thresholds and liminal spaces and all that—but actual doorways. The physical fact of them. A rectangular hole cut into a wall, fitted with a hinged plank, and somehow this absolutely transforms the experience of moving through space. Without a door, a doorway is just an opening, casual and indifferent. Add a door and suddenly there's ceremony. There's the knock. There's the pause before turning the handle. There's the satisfying click when it shuts.

What I find strange is that we've collectively agreed that doors mean something. A closed door says: not now, or: private, or: I'm working, or sometimes: I'm sad and don't want to talk about it. We read these things from a flat piece of wood. We've trained ourselves into this elaborate system of architectural semiotics and we use it constantly without thinking about it.

Children don't understand doors at first. They'll barge into bathrooms without knocking. They'll wander into rooms where adults are having serious conversations. The acquisition of door-etiquette is one of those quiet developmental milestones nobody really celebrates but everyone notices. At some point a child learns to knock, and then they're partway into the strange country of adult social codes, where so much depends on inference and restraint.

I sometimes wonder what other animals make of our obsession with doors. Cats have developed an entire philosophy of resistance to them—they want to be on the other side of any door, regardless of which side they're currently on. There's that famous bit by Schrödinger's-cat-adjacent thinkers about how cats experience doors as fundamental ontological insults. The world should not be partitioned; the world should be one continuous space through which a cat may flow. The door is an affront to feline cosmology.

Dogs, by contrast, seem to accept doors as just one of many baffling human inventions. A dog at a door waits with the patient confusion of someone who has decided not to question the rules. Eventually the door will open. Eventually a human will come or go. The dog files this under: things I do not understand but will tolerate, like vacuum cleaners and the postal service.

I've been reading lately about how the first doors probably weren't doors at all but curtains, hides, woven mats. Something to drape over the opening. The transition from curtain to door must have been gradual—heavier materials, then framed materials, then hinged materials. Somewhere in there our ancestors invented the hinge, which is a genuinely remarkable piece of technology when you think about it. A hinge converts a swing into a controlled motion. It restrains while allowing. It is a small mechanism that makes possible an entire grammar of space.

The Romans had elaborate door cults. Janus, the two-faced god, presided over thresholds and beginnings. January is named for him, because January is the door of the year. The Romans understood that doors were not neutral. They were places where one state of being ended and another began. They built temples whose doors were opened only during wartime. They had gods who specifically oversaw the hinges of doors. Cardea was one—from her name we get "cardinal," because the cardinal points are the hinges on which the world turns.

There's something about this that feels right to me, even though I don't believe in any of it literally. The world does seem to turn on hinges. Lives turn on hinges. You walk through a door one day and your life is different on the other side. The door to a hospital. The door to a job interview. The door to a first date's apartment. We say "when one door closes, another opens" with such reflexive ease that we've worn the metaphor smooth, but the metaphor is doing real work. It's saying: existence has structure, life is partitioned, you pass through phases as you pass through rooms.

I think about the doors I've stood in front of without going through. The ones I knocked on and walked away from. The ones I rehearsed entering and then never did. The doors that, in retrospect, I'm glad I didn't open. The doors I wish I had. There's a whole shadow architecture of unentered rooms in everyone's life, isn't there? Rooms we walked past, rooms we glimpsed through cracked openings, rooms we heard laughter from but didn't feel invited into.

And the doors we slam. The doors we close softly when we're trying to be mature. The doors we leave ajar so the cat can come in. The doors we lock and then immediately doubt whether we locked, so we go back to check, and then walk away, and then doubt again. The hardware store sells deadbolts and chains and keyless entry pads and the entire industry of door security exists because we're collectively a little anxious about what might come through them.

Sliding doors get their own special category. There's a movie called Sliding Doors that turns the concept into a parable about the parallel lives we might have lived. Whether you make the train or don't. Whether the door slides shut before you reach it. The film is okay—not great—but the concept has stuck because we recognize it. Most of us have a sliding-doors moment we can point to, a hinge of our own life, a door that closed or opened at a critical second.

Revolving doors are perhaps the strangest of all. They are doors that are always partly open and partly closed. They are doors that prevent you from ever fully entering or leaving—you're always in transit. Office buildings love them because they reduce drafts. I think they reduce something else too: the ceremony of arrival. You don't really arrive through a revolving door. You're sort of extruded into the lobby. The building processes you.

That might be enough about doors for one sitting. I've run out of doorways to walk through, though I notice I could go on. There's always another threshold to consider. The page itself is a kind of door, isn't it—and we're standing at the closing one now, and you're about to step back through it into whatever you were doing before.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
